title: Economics: prices can rise even when shelves are full
surprising_fact: Inflation is not always a story of shortages. Prices can keep climbing even when goods are available, because expectations and wage setting start chasing future price increases.
explanation:
People often imagine inflation as too many buyers fighting over too few goods, and that does happen. But once businesses expect higher costs and workers expect higher prices, those expectations can feed into contracts, menus, rents, and wage discussions before a fresh shortage even appears. The economy starts pricing in tomorrow's fear today.

That is why central banks care so much about credibility and messaging. If households and firms believe inflation will come back down, they behave differently than if they assume everything will keep getting more expensive forever. Economics gets surprisingly psychological at exactly the moment it looks most numerical.
takeaways: Inflation can persist through expectations, not just physical scarcity.
Credibility from institutions affects real-world pricing behavior.
Economic outcomes are partly driven by what people think others will do next.
